mnemonics: Orthogonal vectors are like perpendicular building blocks. They provide independent directions, making calculations (like finding coordinates) clean and simple.
An Orthonormal Basis is your 'perfect ruler'—a set of unit-length, perpendicular axes that make measuring (projecting) onto any direction straightforward and error-free.
keyPoints: A set of vectors is orthogonal if every pair of distinct vectors is perpendicular (their dot product is zero).
An orthogonal set where every vector has a length of 1 (unit vector) is called an orthonormal set.

An orthogonal basis for a subspace is a basis that is also an orthogonal set. Any vector in the subspace can be written as a unique linear combination of these basis vectors, and the coefficients are found by simple projection formulas (no need to solve a system of equations).
commonMistakes: Assuming any set of non-zero orthogonal vectors is a basis. They must also span the entire subspace in question to be a basis for it.
Forgetting to normalize the basis vectors when an orthonormal basis is required (e.g., for the Gram-Schmidt process output).
Mistaking linear independence for orthogonality. All orthogonal sets (of non-zero vectors) are linearly independent, but not all linearly independent sets are orthogonal.
